text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to perform a minimally invasive umbilical hernia repair. An incision is made in the peritoneum proximal to the camera to access the preperitoneal space. The preperitoneum is dissected to reveal the hernia defect. The hernia sac is encircled and amputated from the base of the umbilicus, with posterior retraction inverting the sac.

The surgeon continues distal dissection laterally to separate the peritoneum from the posterior rectal sheath. The hernia defect is closed using a zero PDS strata fixed barb suture in a running fashion, preparing for the placement of the physio mesh.

The surgeon places the physio mesh into the dissected space over the closed hernia defect. No fixation sutures are used due to the mesh size, but permanent sutures can attach it to the posterior rectus sheath. A IV-O monocryl Stratafix barbed suture is used to close the peritoneum, excluding the mesh from the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon overlaps the barb suture to lock it in place, completing the preperitoneal placement of the mesh, which is visually separated from the bowel.
